SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper specifies clear model equations (Eq. 1 and 2) for composition and attention and provides end-to-end implementation details (DyNet, preprocessing, hyperparameter search) that support reproducibility. Experimental claims are backed by multi-corpus comparisons (references to Yang et al., Bhatia et al., etc.) and an ablation (UNLABELED vs FULL) and a sensitivity observation about RST treebank size. However, the excerpt does not report statistical significance tests or a detailed analysis of parser-induced errors, leaving minor gaps in experimental rigor that do not overturn the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The main idea—building a recursive neural network over a discourse dependency tree with a non-normalized attention mechanism and relation-specific composition matrices—is a non-trivial extension of prior work. The paper clearly contrasts its learned attention and relation-specific composition with previous hand-crafted weighting schemes (Bhatia et al., Hogenboom et al.) and with normalized attention (Bahdanau et al.), which demonstrates a distinct contribution. The approach is novel enough to interest experts, though it builds on several established components (RST parsing, recursive NNs, attention).

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized logically (background, model, implementation, related work) and defines key notation (e_i, v_i, α_{i,j}) with accompanying figures (Fig.1/2) illustrating the tree and network. Implementation and preprocessing details are described concretely, aiding understanding. A few redundant phrases appear in the provided excerpt, but overall the presentation is coherent and notation consistent.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The work evaluates the model on multiple datasets, conducts hyperparameter search, compares labeled vs unlabeled variants, and reports sensitivity to the size of the discourse treebank, indicating substantial empirical effort. Related work coverage is thorough and the paper supplies practical implementation details (parser used, embeddings, training tricks) that support reproducibility. Missing from the excerpt are detailed error analyses, per-dataset numeric results in this snippet, and explicit statistical tests, so some deeper analyses are absent.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses the important task of leveraging discourse structure for text categorization and demonstrates an approach that could influence future work on discourse-aware models. However, the contribution is an incremental-but-useful advancement rather than a transformative shift, and reliance on an external discourse parser (trained on a small RST treebank) may limit immediate broad applicability.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (discourse-aware neural models for text categorization) is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues, and the methods, baselines, datasets, and evaluation standards discussed (hierarchical attention, GloVe, DyNet, standard corpora) match expectations for such venues. The paper is positioned and motivated appropriately for the community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against relevant and recent baselines (Yang et al. 2016 hierarchical attention networks, Bhatia et al. 2015, Hogenboom et al., Yogatama and Smith) and uses common datasets (e.g., Zhang et al.'s preprocessed dataset, MFC, congressional and movie review corpora), suggesting fair benchmarking. The manuscript acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably and conducts ablations (UNLABELED vs FULL), but the excerpt lacks explicit statements about matched training conditions and statistical significance, leaving small questions about absolute fairness.
